segments.title: Modern armed merchant cruiser practicality
segments.summary: The episode argues that modern armed merchant cruiser concepts are limited by detectability, crew control, weapons handling, and thin merchant-fleet slack.
segments.body: The discussion captures the episode's organizing assessment of containerized weapons and merchant-ship conversions. Dr. Clarke treats the heavily advertised container-ship weapon concept as more plausible as marketing or a narrow special-purpose missile trick than as an efficient major-power substitute for warships, then broadens the discussion to crew control, loading errors, aviation and drone handling, prize-law and commerce-raiding history, mine-sweeping roles, and the shortage of spare merchant shipping in just-in-time logistics.

evidence.note: The opening frames the question around modern merchant cruisers and argues that a visible container-ship missile fit is a poor major-power substitute for existing covert strike options.
The crew-control section explains why a covert merchant conversion would need reliable control over engineering, deck, bridge, and steward personnel.
The missile-container section says the advertised system is not practically convincing and that small-power use still depends on cost, storage, manifest control, and loading reliability.
The closing section turns to mine sweeping, USVs, and reefer-ship conversions, then notes that suitable merchant hulls would be in heavy demand.
The conclusion argues that modern merchant fleets lack the spare capacity of the world wars, so taking ships from trade would stress just-in-time logistics.
